speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're getting your editing environment ready in a way that actually works for blind and low-vision contributors. We're focusing on VS Code: how to access it, how to make it screen-reader friendly, how to sign in to GitHub, how to check whether Copilot is available, and how to move around the interface without guessing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I like that this is framed as setup, not as a side quest. Because if your editor is confusing or inaccessible, everything after that gets harder.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. This is foundation work. And there's one practical note right up front: there's a related audio overview for this material along with the next chapter, there are supporting appendices for VS Code reference and screen reader shortcuts, and the most authoritative references are the VS Code documentation on user interface and accessibility.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if a learner wants both the workshop explanation and the official product docs, they should use both.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Also, this is Day 2, Block 1 material, and it assumes you've already done the Day 1 walkthrough in GitHub's browser interface. That matters, because VS Code makes more sense once GitHub.com itself feels familiar.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are we actually trying to be able to do by the end?

speaker: ALEX
text: Launch VS Code, either in the browser with github.dev or on the desktop app, turn on screen reader support, sign in to GitHub, verify whether Copilot is active, and confidently move through the main interface areas like Explorer, the status bar, menus, settings, and keyboard shortcuts.

speaker: ALEX
text: For this workshop, this is a guided setup chapter with one lightweight completion practice. There isn't an automated check, because setup state lives on your device and account. So instead, your evidence is a structured completion comment on your assigned challenge issue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the pattern here is: open, configure, navigate, verify.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The single practice is your accessibility baseline. You open VS Code in github.dev or desktop, enable screen reader mode, sign in to GitHub, check Copilot status, and navigate core surfaces.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's walk through that like we're doing it together.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start on your Learning Room repository on GitHub.com. Then press the period key. Just a plain dot. On a repository code page, that launches github.dev, which is a browser-based version of VS Code. Give it a few seconds to load.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if pressing period does nothing?

speaker: ALEX
text: That usually means you aren't on the repository's main code page. Maybe you're inside an issue or pull request. The shortcut works on repository code pages.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Once github.dev opens, accessibility comes first, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Always. On Windows with NVDA or JAWS, press Shift+Alt+F1 to enable screen reader mode. You should hear an announcement confirming it. On Mac with VoiceOver, the environment is often already reasonably optimized, but if navigation feels off, open the Command Palette with Command+Shift+P and run the command called Toggle Screen Reader Accessibility Mode.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then Explorer?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Open Explorer with Control+Shift+E on Windows or Command+Shift+E on Mac. Your screen reader should announce the file tree. Use the arrow keys to move through files, find README.md, and press Enter to open it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then there's a really useful navigation trick for long Markdown files.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right: outline or symbols. Press Control+Shift+O, or Command+Shift+O on Mac. In a Markdown file, that gives you headings and sections. For screen reader users, that becomes one of the fastest ways to jump around a document.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then the Command Palette.

speaker: ALEX
text: Open it with Control+Shift+P or Command+Shift+P. Type a command, like Toggle Word Wrap, and run it. Or press Escape to back out. The key idea is that the Command Palette gives you keyboard access to nearly everything in VS Code.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And after that we verify account and tool state.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Find the Accounts button, which is typically near the bottom left area of the interface. If you're signed in, your screen reader should announce your GitHub username. If not, activate it and sign in. Then check the status bar at the bottom and move through it to look for GitHub Copilot status.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Though there's a catch there.

speaker: ALEX
text: There is. github.dev does not support Copilot the same way desktop VS Code does. So if you don't find Copilot in github.dev, that may be expected. Desktop VS Code or a Codespace is where Copilot support is more complete.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What counts as done for the practice?

speaker: ALEX
text: You're done when you've opened github.dev, enabled screen reader mode, signed in to GitHub, checked Copilot status, opened a file, viewed its outline, and run a command from the Command Palette.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then comes the evidence comment.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Go back to GitHub.com, open your assigned setup or readiness issue, and post a completion comment listing whether you opened github.dev, enabled screen reader mode, signed in to GitHub, checked Copilot status, opened a file in Explorer, opened outline or symbols, and opened the Command Palette. If any answer is no, add a note about where you got stuck. Then close the assigned setup or readiness issue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's helpful because it turns setup into something visible, not vague.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The expected outcomes are practical: you can launch and navigate github.dev or desktop VS Code, you can hear navigation announcements, you're signed in to GitHub, you've verified Copilot or understand its limitations, you can open Explorer, Outline, and Command Palette, and you're ready for the VS Code-based chapters that follow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we pause on the "if you get stuck" moments? Because those are often the real lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. If the period key does nothing, check that you're on the repository page. If the screen reader toggle didn't announce anything, use the Command Palette and search for screen reader commands manually. If Explorer seems empty, the repository may still be loading, so wait a few seconds and try again. If VoiceOver navigation feels wrong, explicitly run the screen reader mode toggle. If you can't find the Accounts button, use the Command Palette and search for Accounts. If Copilot isn't in the status bar, remember github.dev may not support it. If a shortcut fails, the Command Palette is your fallback. And if needed, ask a facilitator for a side-by-side demo and repeat the same steps.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That last part matters. Repetition is not failure. It's skill-building.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. There's also a learning moment here: tool setup is part of contribution skill. A stable, accessible editor reduces stress and improves the quality of your work. And the specific things you just tested are not random. github.dev launch, screen reader mode, GitHub sign-in, Copilot status, Explorer, Outline, and Command Palette are the foundation for the rest of the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's also a reusable learning pattern.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes: open the tool in the simplest way possible, sign in and verify your identity and tools, configure accessibility before doing any work, verify each navigation surface with assistive tech, and record what worked and what didn't.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's answer the bigger question: why use VS Code at all if GitHub's website already lets you do a lot?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because GitHub's browser interface is excellent for reviewing, discussing, and triaging, while VS Code is where you create. On GitHub.com, navigating repositories, reading issues and pull requests, and leaving comments are all very strong. But when you need full editing, richer navigation, live feedback, AI assistance, and tighter Git workflows, VS Code becomes the better workspace.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So for editing Markdown contributions, which is a lot of open source contribution work, VS Code gives you a stronger environment.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You get a real text editor, live preview, Copilot assistance when supported, and less tab switching. You also get earlier feedback. Instead of finding out after you push that something is wrong, you can often hear or see errors while you type.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for screen reader users, that immediate feedback is huge.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. One of the important ideas in the learning cards is that VS Code can announce errors, warnings, and suggestions through accessibility mechanisms like ARIA live regions. That means you don't have to wait until a change reaches GitHub to discover a problem. Also, the Command Palette gives you a universal keyboard entry point to features, and the Explorer sidebar gives you a repository tree similar to GitHub.com, but tuned for editing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before installing anything, there's this bridge tool: github.dev.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Think of github.dev as VS Code in your browser. It lets you try the VS Code interface immediately, inside a repository, without committing to a desktop install first.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That makes it a gentle on-ramp.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. There are three main ways to access it. The fastest is pressing the period key while you're on a repository page. That works on repository code pages. Another method is using the direct URL form by changing a GitHub repository URL to the github.dev version. And another is launching it from the repository page interface itself.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What do you get when it opens?

speaker: ALEX
text: You get a full VS Code-style editor in the browser: file explorer, editor tabs, command palette, settings, and the overall interface model you'll later use in desktop VS Code. It's excellent for orientation, small edits, navigation practice, and learning the environment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And what does it not have?

speaker: ALEX
text: It does not give you the full desktop experience. Some features are limited or missing, especially ones that rely on local tooling or deeper extension support. And, importantly for this workshop, Copilot support is not the same there. So github.dev is a bridge, not a complete replacement.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So when should learners use github.dev, and when should they switch to desktop VS Code?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use github.dev when you want the simplest possible start, when you're learning the interface, when you need quick edits, or when you want to confirm you can navigate the environment before installing anything. Use desktop VS Code when you need richer features, more complete extension support, stronger Git integration, and tools like Copilot in the ways the workshop expects.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's make the screen reader experience in github.dev explicit.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good idea. In github.dev, activate screen reader mode immediately. Don't wait until navigation feels broken. Once it's on, VS Code changes how it presents and announces interface elements to work better with assistive technology. Navigation becomes more predictable, announcements become more informative, and moving among panes, trees, editors, and controls makes more sense.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Anything platform-specific there?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. NVDA and JAWS users on Windows should use the direct keyboard toggle when available and listen for confirmation. VoiceOver users on Mac may find things mostly optimized already, but if behavior seems inconsistent, explicitly toggling screen reader accessibility mode through the Command Palette often improves the experience.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's a "try it right now" spirit to this whole section.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Don't just hear about github.dev. Open a repository, press period, enable accessibility mode, and move around. The fastest way to reduce mystery is direct contact.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's broaden from github.dev to VS Code generally. What is screen reader mode in VS Code actually doing?

speaker: ALEX
text: Screen reader mode is VS Code's accessibility layer for nonvisual navigation and feedback. It changes how the app exposes information, how much is announced, and how certain interactions are presented so screen readers can work more effectively.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do learners activate it?

speaker: ALEX
text: On Windows, a common shortcut is Shift+Alt+F1. On any platform, you can always open the Command Palette and search for the command to toggle screen reader accessibility mode. That fallback matters because shortcuts can vary by setup.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And how do you verify it's active?

speaker: ALEX
text: Listen for the confirmation announcement. Then test something concrete: open Explorer, move through a tree, switch focus between interface regions, or navigate a file outline. If those interactions become clearer and more consistently announced, that's a strong sign it's on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What changes once the mode is active?

speaker: ALEX
text: VS Code becomes much more intentional about accessibility output. Focus changes are clearer, tree views and lists are easier to interpret, announcements provide more context, and in general the editor behaves more like a well-structured application rather than a visually optimized one that a screen reader is trying to interpret after the fact.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The source also calls out platform-specific settings. What should learners take from that?

speaker: ALEX
text: That accessibility is not one-size-fits-all. NVDA users may need settings that reduce friction with browse mode or improve spoken feedback in VS Code. JAWS users may have their own preferred interaction patterns and may benefit from explicitly confirming how virtual cursor behavior interacts with the app. VoiceOver users on macOS may need to verify the screen reader mode toggle and adapt to Mac-specific focus conventions. The deeper point is: tune VS Code to your screen reader, not just to generic defaults.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's do the interface tour. If someone opens VS Code and feels overwhelmed, what should they picture first?

speaker: ALEX
text: Picture five major regions. First, the activity bar or primary navigation area that gives quick access to views like Explorer and Search. Second, the side bar, where the selected view's content appears, such as the file tree in Explorer. Third, the editor area, where your files open and where you do most writing and editing. Fourth, panels or auxiliary areas that can show things like output or other tools, depending on configuration. And fifth, the status bar along the bottom, which reports state and provides quick-access controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That already makes it less scary. Not a thousand mystery elements, just a few major zones.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Once learners understand the regions, navigation becomes purposeful. You aren't wandering. You're moving from region to region.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So how do you navigate between regions efficiently?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use direct shortcuts whenever possible. Explorer has a shortcut. The Command Palette has a shortcut. Outline or symbols has a shortcut. Menus can be reached from the keyboard. Settings and keyboard shortcuts have commands. Screen reader users often do best when they rely on known jumps instead of trying to tab through every control in order.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learning card idea here is basically: know the landmarks.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Find your way around by learning the interface landmarks and a few dependable shortcuts. Once you know where Explorer, the editor, status bar, accounts area, and menus live conceptually, VS Code becomes much less cognitively heavy.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Speaking of identity and setup, let's talk about the Accounts button and signing in to GitHub.

speaker: ALEX
text: This matters because many GitHub-connected features in VS Code depend on authentication. Signing in connects your editor to your GitHub account so repository actions, account-aware features, and tools tied to your identity can work smoothly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you sign in?

speaker: ALEX
text: One path is through the Accounts button in the lower-left area. Activate it, choose the GitHub sign-in option, and follow the browser-based authorization flow. Another path is through the Command Palette: search for sign-in or accounts-related commands and launch the sign-in flow from there.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you verify that it worked?

speaker: ALEX
text: Return to the Accounts control and listen for your GitHub username, or otherwise confirm that the interface now reflects your signed-in state. The important thing is to verify, not assume.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learning card summary would be: sign-in is not decorative. It unlocks the account-connected workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Next up: GitHub Copilot status. This is one of those features learners hear about a lot, but they're often unsure whether it's actually on.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's why verification matters. There are three good ways to check. First, the status bar may include a Copilot indicator. Second, the Command Palette may show Copilot commands, which is another clue that the feature is installed and available. Third, a quick test in an editable file may reveal whether suggestions are appearing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if it's not working?

speaker: ALEX
text: Troubleshoot systematically. Confirm you're signed in to the correct GitHub account. Confirm you're in an environment that supports Copilot, usually desktop VS Code rather than github.dev. Check whether the extension or feature is present and enabled. If the status bar doesn't mention it and commands aren't showing, you may not have the supported environment or active setup yet.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learning card here is basically: don't guess based on marketing. Check actual indicators.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly right.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's spend a moment on the status bar itself, because beginners often ignore it.

speaker: ALEX
text: And they shouldn't. The status bar is one of VS Code's most useful summary surfaces. Left to right, it can contain source control or branch information, sync and environment indicators, encoding or line-ending details, language mode, position in the file, accessibility-related state, extension indicators, and sometimes GitHub-connected status such as Copilot.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it's not just decorative chrome. It's operational information.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And for keyboard users, you should practice navigating it intentionally. Use keyboard movement to enter it and move across its items. The point is to learn that important state lives there and can be accessed without a mouse.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This seems especially useful for low-vision users too, because the status bar often centralizes tiny but important details.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. It's a compact dashboard. Learn to consult it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now the menu bar. Some people come to VS Code expecting everything to be hidden behind modern panels, but the menu bar still matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. The menu bar provides the classic structured entry points to commands: File, Edit, Selection, View, Go, Run, Terminal, Help, and related menus depending on platform. For many learners, especially when memorizing shortcuts is still in progress, menus provide a discoverable path.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And how do you access it from the keyboard?

speaker: ALEX
text: That depends somewhat on platform, but the key idea is that you can reach it without a mouse, open the menus, and arrow through commands. Even if you prefer the Command Palette later, the menu bar remains a valuable backup and discovery tool.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learning card is: when you don't know the command name yet, menus help you browse the landscape.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's talk about settings sync, because once learners get things accessible, they really don't want to redo it all on every machine.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's the big advantage. Settings Sync can carry your configuration across environments. What gets synced can include settings, keybindings, extensions, and related preferences, depending on what you choose to include.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you enable it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Typically through the account and settings flow in VS Code. You sign in, turn on sync, and choose what categories to synchronize. The important idea is intentionality: decide what should travel with you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about conflicts?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sometimes two environments disagree. Conflict resolution is the process of choosing which version wins for a given setting or configuration. Learners should know that sync is powerful, but not magical. If something changes unexpectedly, check whether a synced setting overrode a local one.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then there are profiles, which are almost like named bundles of setup.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Profiles let you create distinct configurations for different contexts. You might have one profile for this workshop, another for general development, and another for experimentation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you create one?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use the profiles feature in VS Code to create a new profile, give it a meaningful name, and then customize its settings, extensions, and behaviors.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And switching profiles?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can change from one profile to another through the profiles interface. That allows you to move between setups without manually undoing settings each time.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you recommend for this workshop?

speaker: ALEX
text: A dedicated workshop profile is often a smart move. Keep the accessibility settings, shortcuts, and extensions you need for this class together so you can return to a known-good environment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can profiles be shared?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, profiles can be exported and shared, which can be helpful when facilitators want everyone to start from a common baseline or when peers want to compare setups.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And how do profiles relate to sync?

speaker: ALEX
text: They interact. Settings Sync can preserve and propagate profile-related configuration, but you should still understand which profile you're in and what exactly is being synchronized. The main lesson is: profiles give structure to your setup, and sync helps preserve it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's open the Settings Editor. A lot of learners know settings exist, but they're not always sure how to work with them accessibly.

speaker: ALEX
text: The Settings Editor is the interface for browsing and changing VS Code settings in a more user-friendly way than editing raw JSON by hand. You can open it through menus, commands, or keyboard shortcuts.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Once you're inside, what's the best way to navigate?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use search. The settings surface can be huge, so searching by concept is often better than browsing endlessly. Type terms like accessibility, screen reader, word wrap, minimap, or sync. Results are grouped and can usually be navigated as structured controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The source also mentions special search filters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Filters help narrow what you're looking at, such as user versus workspace settings or particular categories. The lesson for learners is that good search plus filters turns settings from overwhelming into manageable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are the key accessibility settings to configure?

speaker: ALEX
text: At minimum, anything related to screen reader support, editor behaviors that affect navigation, and visual complexity settings that may help low-vision users. The exact choices vary by learner, but the chapter's message is clear: configure accessibility intentionally instead of accepting defaults that may not suit your assistive technology.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learning card would be: search first, filter second, configure deliberately.

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfectly said.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then we have the Keyboard Shortcuts Editor, which is wonderful if you rely heavily on the keyboard.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. This editor lets you inspect, search, and customize commands and their shortcuts. It's a central tool for making VS Code fit your hands and workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you open it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Through the command system, menus, or shortcut pathways in VS Code. Once it opens, search is again your friend. Look up commands by name, like Explorer, Command Palette, screen reader mode, settings, or outline.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if a shortcut conflicts with something else or just feels awkward?

speaker: ALEX
text: Customize it. That's the beauty of the Keyboard Shortcuts Editor. You can bind a command to a different key combination that better matches your memory, your screen reader conventions, or your physical comfort.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels especially important for accessibility, because standard shortcuts are not always ideal for every assistive technology setup.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Customization is not indulgence. It can be the difference between friction and fluency.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we wrap, I want to acknowledge something from the table of contents. It points to more advanced topics like essential keyboard navigation, problems, terminal, Copilot Chat, accessible help and accessible diff, accessibility signals, speech, and Git operations inside VS Code.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Those are important, but this chapter deliberately stays focused on interface and setup. The deep-dive accessibility topics continue in the next chapter. Here, the goal is readiness.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's even an appendix and source trail for people who want to go deeper.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. There are related appendices for reference and screen reader support, plus authoritative source links and a section-level source map pointing back to the official docs. That matters because tools evolve, and official documentation is where learners can confirm current behavior.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if we boil today down to a practical checklist, what is it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Open VS Code in the simplest way you can, usually github.dev from a repository page. Turn on screen reader mode right away. Open Explorer and prove you can navigate the file tree. Open a file. Use outline or symbols to navigate structure. Open the Command Palette and run a command. Sign in to GitHub and verify the account state. Check whether Copilot is available in your current environment. Learn the major interface regions. Know that the status bar and menu bar are useful, not optional. Use Settings Sync and Profiles to preserve an accessible setup. And remember that the Settings Editor and Keyboard Shortcuts Editor are where VS Code becomes yours.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a strong foundation.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. And once that foundation is stable, contributing becomes less about wrestling the tool and more about doing the work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Thanks everyone. If a step didn't work the first time, document it in your evidence comment. That's useful information, not a failure report.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Your accessible development environment is part of your contribution practice. Build it with care, verify it works, and then move forward with confidence.
